content.text: Why Model Routing Requires an ADR

A GenAI application that routes requests across multiple model tiers — or falls back to an alternative model when the primary is unavailable — is making a set of coupled architectural decisions that will affect cost, quality, latency, and reliability for the lifetime of the system. Each routing decision embeds assumptions: that the cheap model is good enough for simple requests, that the premium model is worth the cost for complex ones, that the fallback behaviour is acceptable to users, and that the routing classifier itself is accurate enough not to misroute at meaningful rates.

These assumptions deserve an Architecture Decision Record because they are hard to reverse once embedded in production code and operational contracts. A routing strategy that works at 10 RPS may break at 100 RPS when rate limits are reached simultaneously across tiers. A fallback to a cached response that was acceptable during a demo may be unacceptable for users expecting real-time claim status. Writing the ADR before implementation forces these questions to be answered explicitly.

attrs.alt: Three-tier model routing with per-tier fallback chains

content.text: The Three Routing Patterns Worth Documenting
Production GenAI systems use one or more of three routing architectures. Each has a distinct set of ADR questions.
Pattern 1: Complexity-Based Tier Routing

The most common routing pattern: classify each incoming request by complexity and route it to the cheapest model tier that can handle it adequately. A triage classifier — itself a model or a rule-based heuristic — assigns a tier, and the router dispatches accordingly.

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

class RequestTier(str, Enum):
    SIMPLE = "simple"      # classification, short Q&A, extraction
    STANDARD = "standard"  # summarisation, short generation, RAG answer
    COMPLEX = "complex"    # multi-step reasoning, long-form drafting, analysis

@dataclass
class TierConfig:
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    timeout_ms: int
    cost_per_1k_input: float   # USD
    cost_per_1k_output: float  # USD
    fallback_tier: Optional["RequestTier"] = None

TIER_CONFIG: dict[RequestTier, TierConfig] = {
    RequestTier.SIMPLE: TierConfig(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        max_tokens=512,
        timeout_ms=3000,
        cost_per_1k_input=0.00015,
        cost_per_1k_output=0.00060,
        fallback_tier=None,   # simple has no cheaper fallback
    ),
    RequestTier.STANDARD: TierConfig(
        model="gpt-4o",
        max_tokens=2048,
        timeout_ms=8000,
        cost_per_1k_input=0.00250,
        cost_per_1k_output=0.01000,
        fallback_tier=RequestTier.SIMPLE,
    ),
    RequestTier.COMPLEX: TierConfig(
        model="gpt-4o",          # same model, higher max_tokens
        max_tokens=8192,
        timeout_ms=30000,
        cost_per_1k_input=0.00250,
        cost_per_1k_output=0.01000,
        fallback_tier=RequestTier.STANDARD,
    ),
}

def classify_tier(input_tokens: int, request_type: str) -> RequestTier:
    # Simple rule-based classifier — replace with LLM classifier if accuracy < 0.92
    if request_type in ("classification", "extraction") and input_tokens < 600:
        return RequestTier.SIMPLE
    if input_tokens > 4000 or request_type in ("analysis", "drafting"):
        return RequestTier.COMPLEX
    return RequestTier.STANDARD

def estimate_cost(tier: RequestTier, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    cfg = TIER_CONFIG[tier]
    return (
        input_tokens / 1000 * cfg.cost_per_1k_input
        + output_tokens / 1000 * cfg.cost_per_1k_output
    )

The ADR for this pattern must document the classifier accuracy on a representative sample of production request types. A classifier that routes 15% of standard requests to the simple tier — because it incorrectly scores them as simple — will produce quality regressions that are difficult to detect because no individual response fails hard; quality just degrades gradually.
Pattern 2: Provider Failover Routing

When a single provider's availability or rate limits are insufficient, requests are routed across multiple providers. This pattern requires more careful ADR documentation because the providers may have different strengths, pricing models, data processing agreements, and output format characteristics.

When a failover provider is in a different region or jurisdiction than the primary, the ADR must explicitly document the data residency implication. Activating a fallback that routes GDPR-regulated data to a US provider without documentation is a compliance incident, not just a technical event.
Pattern 3: Quality-Based Shadow Routing

A more sophisticated pattern: run a secondary model in shadow (receiving the same inputs but not serving responses to users) to compare quality and detect when the primary model has degraded. The shadow route can also be used to evaluate a candidate model before promoting it to production.
Shadow mode
Purpose
When to use
Parallel shadow
Compare primary vs candidate model quality in real traffic
Evaluating a model upgrade before promoting; shadow gets same input, responses not served
Scoring shadow
Apply an LLM judge to a sample of primary model responses
Continuous quality monitoring without serving two model responses to users
Cost shadow
Estimate cost of routing more requests to a cheaper tier
Validating cost savings before changing routing thresholds
Latency shadow
Profile latency distribution of a candidate model at real traffic volumes
Before changing timeout values in the routing config
The Model Routing ADR Template

The following template is specialised for routing decisions. Use it in conjunction with the base GenAI ADR template (ADR-001 format) — fill the base sections, then add these routing-specific sections.

# ADR-NNN: [Routing Decision Title]

[... base ADR sections: Status, Date, Deciders, Context, Decision, Alternatives ...]

---

## Routing Topology

Describe the routing graph: what calls what, under what conditions, in what order.

```
Request
  → Classifier (rule-based / LLM-based / heuristic)
      ├── [condition A] → Model Tier 1 (name, version)
      │     └── on failure → Fallback 1a (name) or static response
      ├── [condition B] → Model Tier 2 (name, version)
      │     └── on failure → Tier 1 (degraded mode)
      └── [condition C] → Model Tier 3 (name, version)
            └── on failure → Tier 2 (with human escalation trigger)
```

## Routing Classifier

- Type: [rule-based | heuristic | LLM classifier | ML classifier]
- Accuracy: [measured on sample of N requests; breakdown by tier]
- Misrouting cost: [describe what happens when classifier routes to wrong tier]
- Review trigger: [what metric indicates classifier needs retuning]

## Per-Tier Configuration

| Tier | Model | Max tokens | Timeout (ms) | Cost/req | Rate limit (RPM) |
|------|-------|-----------|--------------|----------|------------------|
| [name] | [model@version] | [N] | [N] | [$X] | [N] |

## Fallback Behaviour

For each failure mode, specify the exact fallback:

| Failure mode | Detection | Fallback action | User-visible effect |
|--------------|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Timeout (>Nms) | AsyncTimeout | Route to tier N-1 | Slightly degraded response |
| Rate limit (429) | HTTP status | Queue with back-pressure | User sees wait indicator |
| Provider outage | error_rate > 5% for 5m | Circuit breaker → static | 'Temporarily unavailable' message |
| Context overflow | HTTP 400 (token limit) | Truncate input and retry | Partial context used; flagged in response |

## Data Residency for Each Route

| Route | Provider | Region | Data residency status | DPA reference |
|-------|----------|--------|-----------------------|---------------|
| Primary | [name] | [region] | GDPR compliant | [DPA link] |
| Fallback | [name] | [region] | [status — flag if non-EU] | [DPA link] |

## Routing Quality Assumptions

- Classifier accuracy: >= [N]% on representative sample of production request types
- Tier 1 quality: [metric] >= [threshold] for [% of requests routed here]
- Tier 2 quality: [metric] >= [threshold] for [% of requests routed here]
- Fallback quality: [what degradation is acceptable; user-facing copy approved by product]

## Cost Model

Projected cost at [N] req/day with this routing configuration:
- [X]% to Tier 1: $[N]/month
- [Y]% to Tier 2: $[N]/month
- [Z]% to Tier 3: $[N]/month
- Total: $[N]/month (vs $[N]/month for single-tier alternative)

## Observability

- Metric: `route_distribution` (% to each tier) | Alert: if Tier 1 share drops below [N]% for 1h (classifier may be broken)
- Metric: `fallback_activation_rate` | Alert: > [N]% for 10m (provider instability)
- Metric: `quality_score_by_tier` | Alert: if Tier 1 score drops below Tier 2 score (routing may be counterproductive)
- Metric: `misrouting_rate` (estimated via shadow) | Alert: > [N]%

## Reversal Cost

[Estimate effort to: change classifier, change tier assignment thresholds, swap provider in one tier, remove a tier]
Worked Example: FNOL Claim Triage Routing ADR

# ADR-002: Two-Tier Model Routing for FNOL Claim Processing

**Status:** Accepted
**Date:** 2024-12-01
**Deciders:** Alice Chen (Architect), David Park (Product), Eve Muller (Platform)
**Review date:** 2025-06-01

## Context

At 4 200 req/day, using gpt-4o for all requests costs £6 200/month (see ADR-001).
Analysis of PoC traffic shows 38% of requests are peril classification only —
short inputs (<600 tokens), single-label output, no drafting required.
These requests do not benefit from gpt-4o's reasoning capability.

## Decision

Route peril classification requests (identified by request_type=classification
and input_tokens < 600) to gpt-4o-mini. Route all other requests to gpt-4o.
Routing uses a rule-based classifier (no additional LLM call required).

## Routing Topology

Request
  → Rule classifier (request_type + token_count)
      ├── classification + tokens < 600 → gpt-4o-mini (Azure West Europe)
      │     └── on failure/timeout → gpt-4o (same region, degraded throughput)
      └── all other request types → gpt-4o (Azure West Europe)
            └── on failure → gpt-4o-mini with "complex request, simplified response" flag
                └── if that also fails → static "Service temporarily unavailable" + ticket

## Routing Classifier

- Type: Rule-based (request_type field + input token count)
- Accuracy: 100% by definition (rules are deterministic); no ML model required
- Misrouting cost: If request_type field is set incorrectly by caller, classification
  request goes to gpt-4o (£0.003 cost increase; no quality impact). Reverse is
  impossible by design (non-classification never routes to mini).
- Review trigger: If classification accuracy drops below 0.87, investigate whether
  mini is still adequate; do not change routing threshold without re-running eval.

## Per-Tier Configuration

| Tier | Model | Max tokens | Timeout | Cost/req | Rate limit |
|------|-------|-----------|---------|----------|------------|
| Classification | gpt-4o-mini@2024-07-18 | 256 | 3 000ms | £0.00012 | 10 000 RPM |
| Standard/Draft | gpt-4o@2024-11-20 | 4 096 | 12 000ms | £0.009 | 3 500 RPM |

## Fallback Behaviour

| Failure mode | Detection | Fallback | User effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| mini timeout (>3s) | AsyncTimeout | Retry once; then route to gpt-4o | +2-4s latency; no quality loss |
| gpt-4o rate limit (429) | HTTP 429 | Queue (max 30s wait) then retry | Wait indicator in UI |
| gpt-4o outage (error_rate>5%) | Alert fires | Static 'claim received' + email | Claim logged; AI response deferred |

## Data Residency

| Route | Provider | Region | GDPR | DPA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| All routes (primary + fallback) | Azure OpenAI | West Europe | Compliant | MSA/DPA v3.1 2024-10-01 |
Note: NO fallback route to US regions. If both Azure West Europe and Azure North Europe
fail simultaneously, activate static fallback only. Do not activate OpenAI direct US endpoint.

## Cost Model (4 200 req/day)

- 38% classification (1 596 req/day) to mini: £0.00012 × 1 596 × 30 = £5.75/month
- 62% standard/draft (2 604 req/day) to gpt-4o: £0.009 × 2 604 × 30 = £703/month
- Total: £709/month vs £6 200/month (single-tier gpt-4o) — 89% cost reduction

## Observability

- `route_pct_mini`: target 35-42%; alert if outside range for 1h (classifier drift)
- `fallback_rate`: alert if > 2% for 10m (provider issue)
- `mini_classification_f1`: target >= 0.87; alert if < 0.83 for 2h
- `cost_per_request_gbp`: alert if > £0.010 (routing may have shifted)
Common ADR Omissions for Routing Decisions
Omission
Why it causes problems
What to document instead
Fallback not specified per failure mode
Team invents fallback behaviour during an incident; different engineers implement it differently
Document fallback for every failure mode: timeout, rate limit, hard error, context overflow
Data residency of fallback route not checked
Activating a US-region fallback silently violates GDPR for EU customer data
List every route with its region and DPA status; flag non-compliant fallbacks as disabled
Routing classifier accuracy not measured
Classifier misroutes 20% of requests; cost or quality impact is attributed to model, not routing
Measure accuracy on a sample of 500+ real requests; set re-evaluation trigger
Cost model assumes fixed routing split
Actual traffic shifts (e.g. more complex queries in Q4 due to seasonal claims); cost doubles unexpectedly
Model cost as a range: base case (current traffic mix) + worst case (100% to premium tier)
No definition of acceptable fallback quality
Fallback response 'claim received, we will contact you' deployed without product approval
Get explicit sign-off on fallback UX copy and quality level; document in ADR
Version pin per tier not documented
Provider updates one tier's model; routing comparison is now between different model versions
Pin model version for every tier; document update policy per tier
Routing ADR Review Triggers
A routing ADR should be reviewed — not just at its scheduled 6-month date — whenever any of the following occur:
The routing classifier accuracy drops below its documented threshold on any tier
A new model tier becomes available at meaningfully lower cost or higher quality
The actual routing split diverges by more than 10 percentage points from the documented model (indicates traffic pattern shift)
A fallback route is activated in production for the first time (validates whether the documented fallback behaviour is correct and acceptable)
A provider changes its pricing model, regional availability, or DPA terms for any route in the topology
An incident is caused by routing behaviour not covered in the ADR's fallback specification
The value of a routing ADR is highest when an incident forces the team to answer 'what should happen when provider X fails?' before it happens. Document the answer now, not during the 3 AM call.
Summary

Model routing and fallback ADR patterns extend the base GenAI ADR template with routing-specific sections: the routing topology graph, per-tier configuration, failure-mode-to-fallback mapping, data residency status for each route, cost modelling at expected traffic splits, and routing-specific observability metrics.

The worked FNOL example demonstrates an 89% cost reduction from a two-tier routing decision — a saving that would not have been captured without the cost model section, and whose data residency constraint (no US fallback routes) would not have been enforced without explicitly documenting it. Routing decisions that are not written down as ADRs tend to be undone or circumvented during incidents by well-meaning engineers who do not know the constraints the original decision was designed to satisfy.
